coredata.dc:title: A versatile fluorimetric chemosensor for mercury (II) assay based on carbon nanodots
coredata.dc:creator: Liang, Xiao-Chun
Chen, Shuqin
Gao, Jianmei
Zhang, Hongyu
Wang, Yu
Wang, Ji-Hui
Feng, Liang
coredata.dc:description: Human urine mercury level functions as a pre-warning signal for mercury explosure, either in acute or chronic events. Unlike common hydrargyrism due to organic mercury uptake overproof via aquatic products, the risk of inorganic mercury toxication would be much higher for residents living in mercury-related industry areas due to direct or indirect mercury wastewater emission to vicinal water system. In this work, we reported a novel fluorimetric chemosensor, 2,6-pyridinedicarboxylic acid-derived carbon nanodots (PECDs), which exhibits excellent versatility in direct inorganic Hg2+ detection. The fabrication was proved facile, inexpensive and eco-friendly. The morphology, chemical and spectral properties of PECDs were thoroughly investigated by using HRTEM, XPS, ATR-FTIR, UV–vis and fluorescent spectrum. PECDs showed a broad range of linearity between fluorescence intensity and Hg2+ concentration. The limit of detection was calculated as low as 75 nM. The sensing mechanism was investigated and assumed as fluorescence quenching originated from the formation of PECDs-Hg2+ complex, which facilitates the non-radiative relaxation of surface emission of PECDs. In Hg2+ assay for real samples, considerable recoveries (RSD < 6%) and good linearity (R2 > 0.99) were obtained in all four kinds of samples, involving reservoir water, tap water, female and male human urines. Thus, we believe our method would make certain contribution to the development of applicable versatile sensing platform for Hg2+.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Mercury
Fluorimetric
Chemosensor
Carbon nanodots

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of Hg2+ in real samples

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported through the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant 21677144 , 31371764 ). The work was also supported by the Chinese Academy of Sciences (100 Talents Program) .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic of the preparation of PECDs and their application for Hg2+ sensing.
Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) of the as-synthesized PECDs. The up inset showed the size distribution of PECDs, and the down inset illustrated the morphology of PECDs.
(A)XPS spectra of the PECDs. (B–D) High-resolution XPS spectra of C1 s (B), N1 s (C) and O1s (D) of the PECDs, respectively.

ATR-FTIR spectrum of the PDCA (blue curve), PECDs(red curve) and PECDs + Hg2+ (black curve). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(A) UV–vis absorption of the PDCA (black) and PECDs (red), photoluminescence excitation (blue) and emission (green) spectra of PECDs in aqueous solutions (50 μg/mL), λex = 340 nm and λem = 420 nm. Insets show the photographs of the obtained PECDs in aqueous solution under illumination of white (left) and UV (365 nm, right) light; (B) Normalized FL spectral of the PECDs at different excitation wavelength. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(A) Fluorescence spectra of PECDs in the prescence of various concentrations of Hg2+ (0, 0.1, 1, 2, 4, 8, 10, 20, 40, 80, 100 μM), (B) Corresponding relationship between F/F0 and the concentrations of Hg2+, (C) UV–vis absorption of PECDs in the absence (black) and presence (red) of Hg2+ (100 μM), (D) The corresponding fluorescence intensities of PECDs towards different ions(100 μM) in the absence (black) and presence (red) of Hg2+ (100 μM). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(A-B) F/F0 of PECDs mixed with different concentrations of Hg2+ (0.1−10 μM) (n = 3) in the nature (A) and tap water (B). (C-D) F/F0 of PECDs mixed with different concentrations of Hg2+ (0.1–8 μM) (n = 3) in the urine samples of Male(C) and Female (D).
Comparison of sensing performance of different fluorescent probes for Hg2+ detection.
Determination and recoveries tests of Hg2+ in the nature water by the present methods (n = 3).
Determination and recoveries tests of Hg2+ in the tap water by the present methods (n = 3).
Determination and recoveries tests of Hg2+ in the urine sample by the present methods (n = 3).

head.ce:title: A versatile fluorimetric chemosensor for mercury (II) assay based on carbon nanodots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Biological Engineering, Dalian Polytechnic University, Dalian, Liaoning 116034, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Lab of Separation Science for Analytical Chemistry, Dalian Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Dalian, Liaoning 116023, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Lab of Separation Science for Analytical Chemistry

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Human urine mercury level functions as a pre-warning signal for mercury explosure, either in acute or chronic events. Unlike common hydrargyrism due to organic mercury uptake overproof via aquatic products, the risk of inorganic mercury toxication would be much higher for residents living in mercury-related industry areas due to direct or indirect mercury wastewater emission to vicinal water system. In this work, we reported a novel fluorimetric chemosensor, 2,6-pyridinedicarboxylic acid-derived carbon nanodots (PECDs), which exhibits excellent versatility in direct inorganic Hg2+ detection. The fabrication was proved facile, inexpensive and eco-friendly. The morphology, chemical and spectral properties of PECDs were thoroughly investigated by using HRTEM, XPS, ATR-FTIR, UV–vis and fluorescent spectrum. PECDs showed a broad range of linearity between fluorescence intensity and Hg2+ concentration. The limit of detection was calculated as low as 75 nM. The sensing mechanism was investigated and assumed as fluorescence quenching originated from the formation of PECDs-Hg2+ complex, which facilitates the non-radiative relaxation of surface emission of PECDs. In Hg2+ assay for real samples, considerable recoveries (RSD < 6%) and good linearity (R2 > 0.99) were obtained in all four kinds of samples, involving reservoir water, tap water, female and male human urines. Thus, we believe our method would make certain contribution to the development of applicable versatile sensing platform for Hg2+.

ce:section.ce:para: Over the past few decades, mercury and its inorganic salts have attracted continuous attentions due to their irreplaceability in various industrial activities with severely neurological, psychological toxicity [1]. More importantly, they also possess high bioavailability through which inorganic mercury salts can convert to more toxic and stable, bio-accumulating organic mercury via the methylation of anaerobic bacteria [2–5]. Unlike common hydrargyrism due to organic mercury uptake overproof via aquatic products, the risk of inorganic mercury toxication would be much higher for residents living in mercury-related industry areas due to direct or indirect mercury wastewater emission to vicinal water system [6].A well quoted example of environmental contamination which led to death of nearly 600 persons took place in Minamata Japan in 1950s [7]. The monitoring of Hg2+ level in various samples that are easily affected by mercury wastewater emission is, therefore, very important in aquatic ecosystems [8,9]. As such, the intracorporal mercury level of correlating residents should also be periodically examined to avoid the unconscious ingestion either through atmospherical or aqueous pathway. A versatile Hg2+ sensing platform for in different media detection is highly desirable.

As it is well known that the ingestion of organic mercury (mainly MeHg) can be detected from both human blood and urine (via demethylation of MeHg during metabolism), whilst exposure to inorganic mercury is predominately accumulated in kidneys and excreted in urine [10], which makes urinary system becomes an importance metabolic pathway of mercury [11,12]. As a result, mercury level in urine has become an important indicator of mercury uptake in human body either via acute or chronic way. The World Health Organization (WHO) has set a threshold limit value of 50 μg/g creatinine in urine for the occupational exposure to mercury, which is corresponded to about 50 μg/L (0.25 μmol/L), the levels of urinary mercury of non-occupational people are below 20 μg/L (0.1 μmol/L), and there are no clinical or subclinical effects when urinary mercury are below 20 μg/L [13]. Additionally, upon collaborating with the assay of homologous blood mercury level, the total mercury urinary level can also provide specific information for searching mercury contamination sources, which would be very helpful for the prevention of further exposure and in clinical therapy.

Comparing to hidebound and jargon-filled instrumental analyses including cold-vapor atomic absorption spectrometry(CV-AAS) [14–16], cold-vapor atomic fluorescence spectrometry (CV-AFS) [17,18], and inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) [19,20], fluorescent chemosensors developed from small molecules, polymers, proteins, DNA/RNA and some nanomaterials exhibit more brilliant perspectives due to their suitability in the establishment of inexpensive, personalized, portable pre-warning apparatus [21–26]. Although legions of chemosensors with splendid sensitivity or selectivity to Hg2+ have been reported, drawbacks from tedious synthesis, low yield, high cost, poor robustness and solubility, or repeated contamination impeded their applicability.

To design a capable and versatile chemosensor for Hg2+, an important step is to select an eligible Hg2+-receptor which generally consists of metal chelators. Amid a variety of potential candidates, 2,6-pyridinedicarboxylic acid (PDCA) is remarkable because PDCA-functionalized golden nanoparticles have been demonstrated with excellent sensitivity and selectivity toward Hg2+ [27–30]. However, this kind of probes also suffered from forgoing problems. A new protocol which can iron out such problems but retain the sensitivity and selectivity of PDCA-based chemosenor, will be highly desirable.

In this work, an intentional manipulation by changing PDCA-based sensor matrix from golden nanoparticles to PDCA-derived carbon nanodots (denoted as PECDs) was employed. On the one hand, carbon nanodots are widely acknowledged as flaring matrices for fluorimetric chemosensors due to their outstanding characteristics involving low-cost, easy-fabrication, prominent solubility, superior chemical and photophysical stability [31–38]. On the other hand, the residual at the surface of as-synthesized PECDs serves the function of natural Hg2+ receptors, giving intrinsic sensitivity and selectivity toward Hg2+ without further surface decoration. For fabrication, PDCA and ethylenediamine were chosen as reactants which firstly supplied sufficient carbon matrix via polymerization before carbonization process. The adoption of one-step solvent-free thermal treatment ensures the simplicity and time-saving characteristics for this chemosensor. After thoroughly determination of spectral properties and sensing ability of as-obtained PECDs, the limit of detection (LOD) was calculated as 75 nM, indicating the considerable sensitivity of PECDs towards Hg2+. The sensing mechanism was assumed as fluorescence quenching originated from the formation of PECDs-Hg2+ complex, which substantially enhanced the non-radiative relaxation rate of surface emission of PECDs [39], as shown in Scheme 1 . In application, we successfully demonstrated the versatility of PECDs in the detection of Hg2+ level in natural water, tap water and human urine samples, where all results exhibited considerable recoveries (RSD < 6%) and excellent linearity with (R2 > 0.99). We believe our method will make certain contribution in the establishment of applicable versatile Hg2+ sensing platform.

Pyridine-2,6-dicarboxylic acid (PDCA) and ethylenediamine anhydrous (C2H8N2) were used to synthesize PECDs. PDCA and CuCl2·2H2O, MgCl2·6H2O, ZnCl2, AlCl3·6H2O, CdCl2, AgNO3, NiCl2·6H2O, CoCl2·6H2O, FeCl3·6H2O were purchased from Alfa Aesar (Shanghai, China). Ethylenediamine anhydrous, NaCl, KCl, CaCl2 and acetate buffer solution (ABS, 0.2 M, pH = 5.6) were obtained from Tianjin Kemiou Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Tianjin, China). HgCl2 were bought from Beijing Chemical Works. Pure-A-Lyzer Dialysis kits (3500 kDa), was purchased from Sigma Aldrich. All reagents were of analytical grade and used as received without further purification. All metal cations stock solutions were prepared by using ABS. Deionized water was used throughout the standard experiments. The natural water samples were collected from Xishan reservoir which is drinking-water resources of Dalian city. The tap water samples were collected from our laboratory (Shahekou district, Dalian). The urine samples were collected from healthy volunteers.

The UV–vis absorption spectra were performed by Persee TU-1901 UV–vis spectrophotometer. Fluorescent spectra were recorded by F-4600 fluorescence spectrophotometer. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were photographed by JEM-2100 microscope in the case of a voltage of 200 kV. The Thermo Nicolet IS50 attenuated total reflectance Fourier transform infrared (ATR-FTIR) spectrometer was used to record infrared spectra of as-prepared PECDs and PDCA. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) of the PECDs was measured by Thermo Scientific K-Alpha instrument.

The PECDs were synthesized through a one-step solvent-free thermal treatment of pyridine-2,6-dicarboxylic acid and ethylenediamine anhydrous. In general, 500 mg pyridine-2,6-dicarboxylic acid and 2 mL ethylenediamine were added into a 20 mL vial,then the mixture was sonicated for 20 min to form a homogeneous solution. After that, the solution was fully transferred to a 25 mL Teflon-lined stainless autoclave, and was heated to 250 °C for 4 h. After the reaction, the autoclave was cooled down to room temperature. Then 10 mL distilled water was added to dissolve the products, and then stirred for a while. The product solution was purified by dialysis against distilled water within a dialysis kit (3500 kDa) for 48 h. The centrifugal treatment was performed to remove unreacted PDCA precipitates and over-carbonized by-products. Supernate was collected and dried to give PECDs. The as-obtained PECDs were re-dispersed in ABS at a concentration of 1 mg/mL for further application.

The quantum yield (QY) of the PECDs was determined and calculated by using quinine sulfate (QY = 0.54 at 360 nm) as reference [40]. The quinine sulfate was dissolved in 0.1 M H2SO4 (refractive index is 1.33). The PECDs were dispersed in ABS (refractive index is 1.33). The absorbance at 360 nm was kept below 0.1 to minimize the re-absorption effect. The quantum yield was then calculated by the following equation:
Q c = Q s ×
F c
×
A S
×
n c 2
F s
×
A c
×
n s 2
Wherein “Q” is the quantum yield, “F” is the integrated emission intensity, “n” is the refractive index of the solvent, and “A” is the absorbance at 360 nm. The subscript “c” and “s” refer to PECDs and quinine sulfate, respectively. The QY of the PECDs was calculated as 4.5%.

Fluorometric detection of Hg2+ was performed in distilled water at room temperature. 50 μL of 1 mg/mL PECDs was added in 850 μL distilled water, and then 100 μL of HgCl2 with different concentrations was added. The mixtures were shaken up and incubated for 10 min to ensure complete reaction. The fluorescent spectra of all samples were recorded at the excitation wavelength of 340 nm.

Thirteen common metal cations were used for interference experiments under the same condition, including K+, Cu2+, Na+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Ca2+, Al3+, Cd2+, Ag+, Ni2+, Co2+, Fe3+, and Hg2+. The concentration of all metal cations was adjusted to 100 μM in the interference experiment. After adding different metal cations, the mixtures were shaken up and incubated for 10 min to ensure complete reaction. In the fluorometric measurement, the excitation wavelength for all samples was 340 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Hg2+ in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: The natural water samples were collected from Xishan reservoir. The samples were centrifuged at 12500 rpm for 20 min and then filtered through a cellulose acetate membrane with 0.22 μm pore diameter. The resultant water samples were spiked with Hg2+ at different concentrations as stock solutions for further use.

The tap water samples were firstly centrifuged at 12500 rpm for 20 min to remove large impurities. A 0.22 μm cellulose acetate membrane was used for further purification of tap water sample. The PECDs were dispersed in tap water and spiked with Hg2+, and then incubated for 10 min at the room temperature. The change of fluorescence intensity of mixtures at 420 nm was measured to evaluate practice usage of PECDs for Hg2+ detection in tap water samples.

The urine samples were collected from healthy volunteers. 2.50 mL of each urine sample and 4 mL nitric acid were added in Teflon vessels. The vessels were sealed and put into the microwave digestion system. The parameters set in microwave digestion process were shown in Table S1 (see the Supporting Information, SI). The digested solution was poured in round flask, and the excessive nitric acid was removed by distill method. The exhaust NO2 was bubbling within condensation NaOH solution. Clear solutions were finally obtained after acid removal process. The urine samples were diluted 10-fold. The ABS solution with Hg2+ at different concentrations were spiked into each the urine sample for further experiments.

The morphology and size distribution of PECDs were investigated by Transmission electron microscopy (TEM). As shown in Fig. 1 , the as-prepared PECDs were monodispersed nanoparticles with near spherical morphology (down inset of Fig. 1), and were well separated from each other. The average diameter was determined 5.49 nm, which is similar with CDs previously reported otherwhere [9,41].

In order to obtain specific chemical composition and elemental analysis of PECDs X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) were performed. As shown in Fig. 2 A, PECDs are typical N-doped carbon dots. The C/N/O atom ratio of PECDs was calculated as C16.61:N5.01:O3.38. Since in pure PDCA, the N:O atom ratio would be much lower (N8:O38), ethylenediamine-terminated polymerization (>N20.28:O15.44) was assumed to take place before the carbonization step. The C1 s spectrum showed three peaks at 284.3, 285.4, 287.5 eV which were attributed to CC/CC, CN, CO bonds, respectively (Fig. 2B). The peaks at 399.0, 400.7, 401.0 eV in N1 s spectrum were ascribed to pyridyl N, (CO)(NH)- and NH groups, respectively(Fig. 2C). Only one peak at 531.0 eV was found in O1s spectrum (Fig. 2D), and was assigned to the CO bands. These results strongly demonstrated that amide groups were formed in PECDs.

The infrared spectra were recorded to elucidate the structural difference between precursor, PECDs and PECDs/Hg complex, as shown in Fig. 3 . The formation of −(CO)(NH)- was reflected from the shift of CO stretching vibration peak from 1695 to 1654 cm−1, the presence of strong amide bending peak at 1523 cm−1, the weakening of CO stretching at 1247 cm−1 and hydrogen-bonded OH out of plane bending peak at 921 cm−1, and the replacement of OH stretching at 2539 cm−1 by NH stretching at 3288 cm−1. This matched well with the absent peak represented carboxylic groups and neogenic peak of amide in XPS results. By the comparison of IR spectra of PECDs and PECDs + Hg2+, we found that peaks relating to amines were much altered. For instance, pyridyl N at 742 cm−1 was much weakened with the presence of the motion of NH towards low wave number field from 3288 to 3264 cm−1. Resultantly, peaks correlating to coordinating NH vibration in the range from 2400 to 3200 cm−1 were strengthened (see the SI, Fig. S1). These results imparted that the chelate positions of PECDs with Hg2+ would predominately consist of pyridine-related structures.

The absorption spectrum was recorded, as shown in Fig. 4 A (red line). The 50 μg/mL PECDs in ABS barely showed absorption bands in visible light region, thus leading to transparent appearance (inset of Fig. 4A). Two absorbance peaks centered at around 224 and 275 nm, respectively, could be clearly observed on the absorption spectrum. According to previous literatures about CDs, the peak at 224 nm was ascribed to the π→π* transition at conjugated component of carbon backbone. The other peak at 275 nm could be attributed to electron transition from non-bonding to π* (n → π*) orbital at heteroatoms. Comparing to the absorption peaks of PDCA at 196 nm and 272 nm (in black line in Fig. 4A), broadened absorption band of PECDs imparted two things: 1) the broadened and red-shifted peak at 224 implied that new species containing-π-bond formed during the formation of skeleton of PECDs involving graphite-like components (orderly hexagon arrangement in Fig. 1 inset); 2) the weakened but broadened peak at 275 nm suggested that the chemical environment of heteroatoms is significantly changed after synthesis. This assumption was consistent with the XPS and IR results about the formation of carboxamides and other aromatic heterocycles which resultantly lowered the possibility of n → π* via the decrease of electron long pairs number in skeleton.

On fluorescent spectra, the precursor PDCA exhibits a characterized emission centered at approximate 390 nm under 340 nm excitation. For PECDs, an intense emission band centered at 420 nm with a shoulder around 390 nm could be observed by the same excitation wavelength. This shoulder peak was by no means originated from residue of unreacted precursor, but was proposed stemming from PDCA-resembled component in PECDs matrix due to two reasons: First, PDCA has slight solubility (about 5 g/L, 5‰) in water, which means after two times dialysis (1:100) and following centrifuge treatment, the residual concentration of PDCA would not be higher than 1 μg/mL in as-prepared 50 μg/mL PECDs stock solution. And this theoretical value was evaluated based on the premise that no PDCA was consumed during the reaction. In the experiment, the ultimate PDCA residue can be ignored, since ethylenediamine was exceeding in raw ratio. The second reason was concluded from fluorescence quenching experiment and will be elaborated in the later paragraph.

When exposing PECDs to a series of excitation wavelength ranging from 310 to 400 nm, we found fluorescence intensity maximum was firstly increased, and then decreased gradually accompanied with a considerable red-shift from 410 to 465 nm (Fig. 4B). The excitation maximum of PECDs is observed at 340 nm. According to a recent report which excitation single-dot spectroscopy was performed for analyzing excitation-dependent properties of CDs, it stated that the emission sites are related to the sp2 subregions in the carbon core, as well as the functional groups on the surface. Such excitation-dependent emission property had been widespread reported as an important criteria for CDs species[42–44].

For the purpose of understanding quenching mechanism, we also recorded absorption spectra of PECDs in the presence and absence of Hg2+, as shown in Fig. 5C. Upon the addition of 100 μM Hg2+, the absorption band centered at 224 nm was much strengthened, yet the absorption band centered at 275 nm was nearly unattenuated. This result implied that Hg2+ would be attached to the conjugated domain of PECDs, rather than to the oxygen-related surface groups. Although the photoluminescence mechanism of CDs is still an open controversy, the emission originated from oxygen-related surface groups are thought making major contribution to fluorescence of CDs. In the case of PECDs, the complex formation between PECDs and Hg2+ was assumed taking place at conjugated-amine (primarily pyridyl N) chelating position of PECDs rather than surface groups according to results from absorption and IR spectra. The formed PECDs@Hg2+ complex substantially boosted electrons exciting transition at neogenic conjugated component, and to some extents, suppressed emission at oxygen-related surface groups via the formation of more favorable non-radiative deactivation pathway. This assumption could also explain why Hg2+ could not completely quench the fluorescence of PECDs. Another thing should be noted here is that the decrease of fluorescence intensity of PECDs in the presence of Hg2+ was integrated (shoulder peak with main peak), which could exclude coexisting the residue of PDCA with PECDs in solution as we mentioned in foregoing paragraphs.

The selectivity experiment was performed by using thirteen metal cations as interference, including Hg2+, K+, Cu2+, Na+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Ca2+, Al3+, Cd2+, Ag+, Ni2+, Co2+ and Fe3+. It was seen that the fluorescence intensity changed distinctively upon the addition of different metal ions at the same concentration (100 μM) at excitation wavelength of 340 nm, as shown in Fig. 5D (black). Only the addition of Hg2+ led to a dramatic quenching of fluorescent intensity of PECDs. Although the addition of Cu2+ and Fe3+ also gave rise to certain decrease of fluorescent intensity, the interference experiment results clearly elucidated capability of PECDs for selectively detecting Hg2+, as shown in Fig. 5D (red). In the present other metal cations, the sensitivity of PECDs towards Hg2+ was merely affected, even coexisting with Cu2+ and Fe3+. According to the literature, such outstanding selectivity and specificity of PECDs to Hg2+ could be explained as the formation of more stable complex by PECDs with Hg (II) than with other metal ions due to larger dissociation constants logK [54].

In order to investigate the versatility of PECDs for Hg2+ detection, different real samples including reservoir water, tap water and urine samples were tested[6,9,49]. All conditions were kept the same as those in the standard experiment except the replacement of distilled water by real samples.

For natural and tap water samples, all recoveries were calculated based the results obtained from the addition of 1 μM, 2 μM, 4 μM and 8 μM Hg2+ to reservoir and tap water samples, and were listed in Tables 2 and 3
, respectively. The calculated results from experiments were closing to those initially spiked within samples, and showed excellent linearity with R2=0.99 with small RSD values (lower than 6%), as shown in Fig. 6 A and B. These results indicated that PECDs are capable of detection Hg2+ level in natural and tap water that are easily affected by mercury wastewater emission.

We further detected Hg2+ level in male and female urine samples by using PECDs chemosensor. The preparation of urine samples was demonstrated in experimental section Fluorescence intensity of PECDs in urine samples was decreased with increasing the concentration of Hg2+ from 0.1 to 8 μM. The calibration curves were obtained by plotting the values of F/F0 versus the concentrations of Hg2+ (Fig. 6C and D), and exhibited excellent linearity in both samples (R2=0.9977 for male and R2=0.9997 for female). The recoveries of Hg2+ in urine samples at different concentrations were also calculated and shown in Table 4 . Although the results showed slight fluctuation, the PECDs method for inorganic Hg2+ level detection in urine was clearly feasible.

In summary, we reported a PDCA-based Hg2+-targeted fluorimetric chemosensor based on a one-step, solvent-free thermal treatment of PDCA with ethylenediamine. The morphology and chemical structure of PDCA-based carbon dots (PECDs) were thoroughly investigated by using HRTEM, XPS, and ATR-FTIR analysis. The as-obtained PECDs showed excitation-dependent emission and blue fluorescence in aqueous solution. The sensitivity and selectivity of PECDs towards Hg2+ were thoroughly determined by using fluorescent titration and interference experiments, and the limit of detection (LOD) was calculated as low as 75 nM. In application, PECDs showed competitive sensing ability for Hg2+ in different real samples involving natural water, tap water and human urine samples. We believed that PECDs strategy characterized as easy-fabrication, good-stability and low-price would offer a new approach to establish versatile platform for inorganic Hg2+ detection in real use.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported through the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant 21677144, 31371764). The work was also supported by the Chinese Academy of Sciences (100 Talents Program).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2018.01.182.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Detect, remove and reuse: a new paradigm in sensing and removal of Hg (II) from wastewater via SERS-active ZnO/Ag nanoarrays
Synthesis, characterization and application of poly(acrylamide-co-methylenbisacrylamide) nanocomposite as a colorimetric chemosensor for visual detection of trace levels of Hg and Pb ions
Highly sensitive and reusable mercury (II) sensor based on fluorescence quenching of pyrene moiety in polyacrylamide-based cryogel
Novel selective and sensitive dual colorimetric sensor for mercury and lead ions derived from dithizone-polymeric nanocomposite hybrid
SERS and fluorescence-based ultrasensitive detection of mercury in water
Pyrene tethered imidazole derivative for the qualitative and quantitative detection of mercury present in various matrices
Facile Hg2+ detection in water using fluorescent self-assembled monolayers of a rhodamine-based turn-on chemodosimeter formed via a click reaction
Highly sensitive and selective detection of mercury ions using N, S-codoped graphene quantum dots and its paper strip based sensing application in wastewater
Current progress on understanding the impact of mercury on human health
Interpreting mercury in blood and urine of individual patients
Inorganic and organic mercury in blood, urine and hair in low level mercury vapour exposure
Detection of mercury ions (Hg2+) in urine using a terbium chelate fluorescent probe
Speciation of mercury in fish samples by flow injection catalytic cold vapour atomic absorption spectrometry
Suitable extraction of soils and sediments for mercury species and determination combined with the cold vapor generation atomic absorption spectrometry technique
Thiol-modified magnetic silica sorbent for the determination of trace mercury in environmental water samples coupled with cold vapor atomic absorption spectrometry
Determination of trace inorganic mercury in mineral water by flow injection on-line sorption preconcentration-cold vapor atomic fluorescence spectrometry
Applicability of multisyringe chromatography coupled to cold-vapor atomic fluorescence spectrometry for mercury speciation analysis
Method to reduce the memory effect of mercury in the analysis of fish tissue using inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry
Determination of mercury in fish otoliths by cold vapor generation inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (CVG-ICP-MS)
Small-Molecule fluorescent chemosensors for Hg2+ ion
Impedimetric DNA sensor for detection of Hg2+ and Pb2+
Cleavable molecular beacon for Hg2+ detection based on phosphorothioate RNA modifications
Biomimetic construction of protein-conjugated gold clusters for detecting Hg2+
Recent developments on nanomaterials-based optical sensors for Hg2+ detection

Application of an Hg2+ selective imprinted polymer as a new modifying agent for the preparation of a novel highly selective and sensitive electrochemical sensor for the determination of ultratrace mercury ions
Effects of Mn2+ on oligonucleotide-gold nanoparticle hybrids for colorimetric sensing of Hg2+: Improving colorimetric sensitivity and accelerating color change
Sensitive and selective detection of mercury ions based on papain and 2,6-pyridinedicarboxylic acid functionalized gold nanoparticles
Colorimetric detection of mercury ions based on plasmonic nanoparticles
Selective detection of mercury (II) ion using nonlinear optical properties of gold nanoparticles
Highly photoluminescent pH-independent nitrogen-doped carbon dots for sensitive and selective sensing of p-nitrophenol
Chemistry, biology, and medicine of fluorescent nanomaterials and related systems: new insights into biosensing, bioimaging, genomics, diagnostics, and therapy
Green synthesis of carbon nanodots from cotton for multicolor imaging, patterning, and sensing
Highly luminescent N, S- Co-doped carbon dots and their direct use as mercury(II) sensor
Carbon quantum dots and their applications
Synthesis of photoluminescent carbon dots for the detection of cobalt ions
Novel fluorescent carbonic nanomaterials for sensing and imaging
Water-soluble, nitrogen-doped fluorescent carbon dots for highly sensitive and selective detection of Hg2+ in aqueous solution
Carbon dots-based fluorescent probe for trace Hg2+ detection in water sample
A highly selective and sensitive on-off-on fluorescent probe for detecting Hg(II) based on Au/N-doped carbon quantum dots
Modification-free carbon dots as turn-on fluorescence probe for detection of organophosphorus pesticides

Heterogeneous synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon dots prepared via anhydrous citric acid and melamine for selective and sensitive turn on-off-on detection of Hg (II), glutathione and its cellular imaging
White-emitting carbon dots with long alkyl-chain structure: effective inhibition of aggregation caused quenching effect for label-free imaging of latent fingerprint
A highly selective and sensitive polymer-based OFF-ON fluorescent sensor for Hg2+ detection incorporating salen and perylenyl moieties
Hg(II) sensing based on functionalized carbon dots obtained by direct laser ablation
Optical fiber sensor for Hg(II) based on carbon dots
Fluorescent and colorimetric magnetic microspheres as nanosensors for Hg2+ in aqueous solution prepared by a sol-gel grafting reaction and host-guest interaction
A selective and sensitive optode for determination of Hg2+ ion based on covalent immobilization of thiazole yellow on triacetyl cellulose films
Facile synthesis of highly luminescent co-doped carbon nanodots for rapid, sensitive, and label-free detection of Hg2+
Nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots for highly selective and sensitive detection of Hg (II) ions
A novel fluorescent nanoswitch based on carbon dots for sensitive detection of Hg2+ and I−
Parameters for selective colorimetric sensing of mercury(II) in aqueous solutions using mercaptopropionic acid-modified gold nanoparticles
Gold nanoparticle-based miniaturized nanomaterial surface energy transfer probe for rapid and ultrasensitive detection of mercury in soil, water, and fish

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Xiaochun Liang is currently a master student in Dalian Polytechnic University, and her research focuses on the preparation of nanomaterials.
Shuqin Chen is currently a PhD student in Dalian Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences. Her research focuses on the synthesis of metal clusters.
Jianmei Gao is currently a PhD student in Dalian Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences. Her research focuses on the fabrication of sensors.
Hongyu Zhang received his master degree in 2016 from Dalian Polytechnic University. His research focuses on the synthesis of carbon nanodots.

Yu Wang received his PhD degree in 2014 from Kyungpook National University, and is currently an assistant professor of Dalian Institute of Chemical Physics in Chinese Academy of Sciences. His main research focuses on materials preparation and characterization and their applications in sensing.
Jihui Wang received his PhD degree in 2003 from Chungbuk National University. Currently he is a professor of Dalian Polytechnic University. His main research focuses on food safety control.

Liang Feng received his PhD degree in 2005 from Wuhan University. Currently he is a professor of Dalian Institute of Chemical Physics. His main research focuses on the development of sensors for the determination of environmental pollutants, including toxic gases, heavy-metal ions, poisonous anions, etc.
